𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Two — Part One

The Torah — Second Book

SHEMOT

Names

Chapters 1 through 18

Egypt — The Oppression — Moshe — The Ten Plagues — The Exodus — The Crossing of the Sea

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

4QExoda (4Q13) · 4QExodb (4Q14) · 4QExodc (4Q15) · 4QExodd (4Q16)

4QExode (4Q17) · 4QExodf (4Q18) · 4QpaleoExodm (4Q22)

2QExoda (2Q2) · 7QpapLXXExod (7Q1) — Greek Exodus scroll Cave 7

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Beta Israel Ancient Canon

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitutions

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

THE OPPRESSION IN EGYPT — CHAPTERS 1 THROUGH 4

SHEMOT — CHAPTER 1 The Children of Yisra'EL Multiply — The Oppression Begins

1:1 These are the names of the sons of Yisra'EL who came to Egypt with Yaakov, each with his household:

1:2 Re'uven, Shim'on, Levi, and Yehudah,

1:3 Yissakhar, Zevulun, and Binyamin,

1:4 Dan and Naftali, Gad and Asher.

1:5 All the descendants of Yaakov were seventy persons — Yosef was already in Egypt.

1:6 Then Yosef died, and all his brothers and all that generation.

1:7 But the people of Yisra'EL were fruitful and increased greatly — they multiplied and grew exceedingly strong, so that the land was filled with them.

1:8 Now there arose a new king over Egypt, who did not know Yosef.

1:9 And he said to his people: Behold, the people of Yisra'EL are too many and too mighty for us.

1:10 Come, let us deal shrewdly with them, lest they multiply, and if war breaks out they join our enemies and fight against us and escape from the land.

1:11 Therefore they set taskmasters over them to afflict them with heavy burdens. They built for Pharaoh store cities — Pithom and Ra'amses.

1:12 But the more they were oppressed, the more they multiplied and the more they spread abroad. And the Egyptians were in dread of the people of Yisra'EL.

1:13 So the Egyptians ruthlessly made the people of Yisra'EL work as slaves

1:14 And made their lives bitter with hard service, in mortar and brick, and in all kinds of work in the field. In all their work they ruthlessly made them work as slaves.

1:15 Then the king of Egypt said to the Hebrew midwives, one of whom was named Shifrah and the other Puah:

1:16 When you serve as midwife to the Hebrew women and see them on the birthstool, if it is a son, you shall kill him, but if it is a daughter, she shall live.

1:17 But the midwives feared The Creator and did not do as the king of Egypt commanded them, but let the male children live.

1:18 So the king of Egypt called the midwives and said to them: Why have you done this, and let the male children live?

1:19 The midwives said to Pharaoh: Because the Hebrew women are not like the Egyptian women, for they are vigorous and give birth before the midwife comes to them.

1:20 So The Creator dealt well with the midwives. And the people multiplied and grew very strong.

1:21 And because the midwives feared The Creator, he gave them families.

1:22 Then Pharaoh commanded all his people: Every son that is born to the Hebrews you shall cast into the Nile, but you shall let every daughter live.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QExoda and 4QExodb preserve portions of Chapter 1. The account of the oppression and the midwives is attested in the Cave 4 Exodus manuscripts. 4QpaleoExodm — written in paleo-Hebrew script — is the most extensive Exodus manuscript from Qumran and covers large portions of Chapters 1 through 40.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 1 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in this chapter.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 1:7 — the people of Yisra'EL were fruitful and increased greatly. The language here — fruitful, multiply, fill the land — is the language of the Creation blessing in Bereshit 1:28 and the covenant blessing to Avraham, Yitzhak, and Yaakov. The fulfillment of the covenant promise is happening inside Egyptian slavery. YHWH's blessing is not defeated by Pharaoh's oppression. The two forces run simultaneously in the text.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 1:17 — the midwives feared The Creator and did not do as the king of Egypt commanded. Shifrah and Puah are the first named resisters of imperial power in the Torah. They are midwives — women with no political standing — and they defy Pharaoh directly. Their fear of The Creator overrides their fear of the throne. The ancient record names them when it names no Egyptian official in this chapter. YHWH honors what Pharaoh dishonors.

SHEMOT — CHAPTER 2 The Birth of Moshe — His Flight to Midyan — YHWH Hears

2:1 Now a man from the house of Levi went and took as his wife a Levite woman.

2:2 The woman conceived and bore a son, and when she saw that he was a fine child, she hid him three months.

2:3 When she could hide him no longer, she took for him a basket made of bulrushes and daubed it with bitumen and pitch. She put the child in it and placed it among the reeds by the river bank.

2:4 And his sister stood at a distance to know what would be done to him.

2:5 Now the daughter of Pharaoh came down to bathe at the river, while her young women walked beside the river. She saw the basket among the reeds and sent her servant woman, and she took it.

2:6 When she opened it, she saw the child, and behold, the baby was crying. She took pity on him and said: This is one of the Hebrews' children.

2:7 Then his sister said to Pharaoh's daughter: Shall I go and call you a nurse from the Hebrew women to nurse the child for you?

2:8 And Pharaoh's daughter said to her: Go. So the girl went and called the child's mother.

2:9 And Pharaoh's daughter said to her: Take this child away and nurse him for me, and I will give you your wages. So the woman took the child and nursed him.

2:10 When the child grew older, she brought him to Pharaoh's daughter, and he became her son. She named him Moshe, saying: Because I drew him out of the water.

2:11 One day, when Moshe had grown up, he went out to his people and looked on their burdens, and he saw an Egyptian beating a Hebrew, one of his people.

2:12 He looked this way and that, and seeing no one, he struck down the Egyptian and hid him in the sand.

2:13 When he went out the next day, behold, two Hebrews were struggling together. And he said to the man in the wrong: Why do you strike your companion?

2:14 He answered: Who made you a prince and a judge over us? Do you mean to kill me as you killed the Egyptian? Then Moshe was afraid, and thought: Surely the thing is known.

2:15 When Pharaoh heard of it, he sought to kill Moshe. But Moshe fled from Pharaoh and stayed in the land of Midyan. And he sat down by a well.

2:16 Now the priest of Midyan had seven daughters, and they came and drew water and filled the troughs to water their father's flock.

2:17 The shepherds came and drove them away, but Moshe stood up and saved them and watered their flock.

2:18 When they came home to their father Re'uel, he said: How is it that you have come home so soon today?

2:19 They said: An Egyptian delivered us out of the hand of the shepherds and even drew water for us and watered the flock.

2:20 He said to his daughters: And where is he? Why have you left the man? Call him, that he may eat bread.

2:21 And Moshe was content to dwell with the man, and he gave Moshe his daughter Tzipporah.

2:22 She gave birth to a son, and he called his name Gershom, for he said: I have been a sojourner in a foreign land.

2:23 During those many days the king of Egypt died, and the people of Yisra'EL groaned because of their slavery and cried out for help. Their cry for rescue from slavery came up to The Creator.

2:24 And The Creator heard their groaning, and The Creator remembered his covenant with Avraham, with Yitzhak, and with Yaakov.

2:25 The Creator saw the people of Yisra'EL — and The Creator knew.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QExoda and 4QpaleoExodm preserve portions of Chapter 2. The birth and rescue of Moshe are attested in the Cave 4 manuscripts.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 2 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in this chapter.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 2:24-25 — The Creator heard their groaning, and The Creator remembered his covenant, and The Creator saw the people of Yisra'EL — and The Creator knew. Four verbs in two verses: heard, remembered, saw, knew. The Exodus does not begin with a plague or a burning bush. It begins with YHWH hearing, remembering, seeing, and knowing. The covenant made with Avraham in Bereshit 15 is the engine. YHWH remembered it. Everything that follows in Shemot is the outworking of that memory.

SHEMOT — CHAPTER 3 The Burning Bush — The Name of YHWH — The Commission of Moshe

3:1 Now Moshe was keeping the flock of his father-in-law Yitro, the priest of Midyan, and he led his flock to the west side of the wilderness and came to Horev, the mountain of The Creator.

3:2 And the angel of YHWH appeared to him in a flame of fire out of the midst of a bush. He looked, and behold, the bush was burning, yet it was not consumed.

3:3 And Moshe said: I will turn aside to see this great sight, why the bush is not burned.

3:4 When YHWH saw that he turned aside to see, The Creator called to him out of the bush: Moshe, Moshe. And he said: Here I am.

3:5 Then he said: Do not come near — take your sandals off your feet, for the place on which you are standing is holy ground.

3:6 And he said: I am The Creator of your father, The Creator of Avraham, The Creator of Yitzhak, and The Creator of Yaakov. And Moshe hid his face, for he was afraid to look at The Creator.

3:7 Then YHWH said: I have surely seen the affliction of my people who are in Egypt and have heard their cry because of their taskmasters. I know their sufferings,

3:8 And I have come down to deliver them out of the hand of the Egyptians and to bring them up out of that land to a good and broad land, a land flowing with milk and honey, to the place of the Kena'ani, the Hittites, the Amorites, the Perizzites, the Hivites, and the Yevusim.

3:9 And now, behold, the cry of the people of Yisra'EL has come to me, and I have also seen the oppression with which the Egyptians oppress them.

3:10 Come, I will send you to Pharaoh that you may bring my people, the children of Yisra'EL, out of Egypt.

3:11 But Moshe said to The Creator: Who am I that I should go to Pharaoh and bring the children of Yisra'EL out of Egypt?

3:12 He said: But I will be with you, and this shall be the sign for you, that I have sent you: when you have brought the people out of Egypt, you shall serve The Creator on this mountain.

3:13 Then Moshe said to The Creator: If I come to the people of Yisra'EL and say to them, The Creator of your fathers has sent me to you, and they ask me, What is his name? — what shall I say to them?

3:14 The Creator said to Moshe: I AM WHO I AM. And he said: Say this to the people of Yisra'EL — I AM has sent me to you.

3:15 The Creator also said to Moshe: Say this to the people of Yisra'EL — YHWH, The Creator of your fathers, The Creator of Avraham, The Creator of Yitzhak, and The Creator of Yaakov, has sent me to you. This is my name forever, and thus I am to be remembered throughout all generations.

3:16 Go and gather the elders of Yisra'EL together and say to them: YHWH, The Creator of your fathers, The Creator of Avraham, of Yitzhak, and of Yaakov, has appeared to me, saying: I have observed you and what has been done to you in Egypt.

3:17 And I promise that I will bring you up out of the affliction of Egypt to the land of the Kena'ani, the Hittites, the Amorites, the Perizzites, the Hivites, and the Yevusim — a land flowing with milk and honey.

3:18 And they will listen to your voice, and you and the elders of Yisra'EL shall go to the king of Egypt and say to him: YHWH, The Creator of the Hebrews, has met with us — and now, please let us go a three days' journey into the wilderness, that we may sacrifice to YHWH our Creator.

3:19 But I know that the king of Egypt will not let you go unless compelled by a mighty hand.

3:20 So I will stretch out my hand and strike Egypt with all the wonders that I will do in it. After that he will let you go.

3:21 And I will give this people favor in the sight of the Egyptians. And when you go, you shall not go empty,

3:22 But each woman shall ask of her neighbor, and any woman who lives in her house, for silver and gold jewelry, and for clothing. You shall put them on your sons and on your daughters. So you shall plunder the Egyptians.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QExoda, 4QExodb, and 4QpaleoExodm preserve portions of Chapter 3. The burning bush account and the revelation of the divine name are attested in the Cave 4 manuscripts. The divine name as stated in verse 3:14 — Ehyeh Asher Ehyeh, I AM WHO I AM — is preserved in the DSS text.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 3 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in this chapter.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3:14 — I AM WHO I AM. The self-declaration of YHWH at the burning bush is the foundational divine name revelation of the entire Torah. The Hebrew Ehyeh Asher Ehyeh — I AM WHO I AM, or I WILL BE WHO I WILL BE — is rooted in the verb hayah, to be or to become. YHWH is not static being — YHWH is dynamic, self-defining, self-sustaining existence. The name is not given to be analyzed into a philosophical category. It is given as a commission: this name shall be remembered throughout all generations — verse 3:15. The four-letter name YHWH — 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 in the ancient Paleo-Hebrew script — derives from this same verb root.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3:7 — I have surely seen the affliction of my people. YHWH uses the first person possessive: my people. Not Avraham's people. Not the Hebrew people. My people. The covenant language of ownership and responsibility runs from YHWH's side. Before Moshe accepts the commission, before the plagues begin, before any miracle occurs — YHWH has already declared the people his own.

SHEMOT — CHAPTER 4 The Signs Given to Moshe — Aharon Sent — Moshe Returns to Egypt

4:1 Then Moshe answered: But behold, they will not believe me or listen to my voice, for they will say: YHWH did not appear to you.

4:2 YHWH said to him: What is that in your hand? He said: A staff.

4:3 And he said: Throw it on the ground. So he threw it on the ground, and it became a serpent, and Moshe ran from it.

4:4 But YHWH said to Moshe: Put out your hand and catch it by the tail — so he put out his hand and caught it, and it became a staff in his hand —

4:5 That they may believe that YHWH, The Creator of their fathers, The Creator of Avraham, The Creator of Yitzhak, and The Creator of Yaakov, has appeared to you.

4:6 Again YHWH said to him: Put your hand inside your cloak. And he put his hand inside his cloak, and when he took it out, behold, his hand was leprous like snow.

4:7 Then YHWH said: Put your hand back inside your cloak. So he put his hand back inside his cloak, and when he took it out, behold, it was restored like the rest of his flesh.

4:10 But Moshe said to YHWH: O my Lord, I am not eloquent, either in the past or since you have spoken to your servant, but I am slow of speech and of tongue.

4:11 Then YHWH said to him: Who has made man's mouth? Who makes him mute, or deaf, or seeing, or blind? Is it not I, YHWH?

4:12 Now therefore go, and I will be with your mouth and teach you what you shall speak.

4:13 But he said: Oh, my Lord, please send someone else.

4:14 Then the anger of YHWH was kindled against Moshe and he said: Is there not Aharon, your brother, the Levite? I know that he can speak well. Behold, he is coming out to meet you, and when he sees you, he will be glad in his heart.

4:17 And take in your hand this staff, with which you shall do the signs.

4:19 And YHWH said to Moshe in Midyan: Go back to Egypt, for all the men who were seeking your life are dead.

4:20 So Moshe took his wife and his sons and had them ride on a donkey, and went back to the land of Egypt. And Moshe took the staff of The Creator in his hand.

4:21 And YHWH said to Moshe: When you go back to Egypt, see that you do before Pharaoh all the miracles that I have put in your power. But I will harden his heart, so that he will not let the people go.

4:22 Then you shall say to Pharaoh: Thus says YHWH — Yisra'EL is my son, my firstborn.

4:23 And I say to you: Let my son go that he may serve me. If you refuse to let him go, behold, I will kill your firstborn son.

4:27 YHWH said to Aharon: Go into the wilderness to meet Moshe. So he went and met him at the mountain of The Creator and kissed him.

4:28 And Moshe told Aharon all the words of YHWH with which he had sent him to speak, and all the signs that he had commanded him to do.

4:29 Then Moshe and Aharon went and gathered together all the elders of the people of Yisra'EL.

4:30 Aharon spoke all the words that YHWH had spoken to Moshe and did the signs in the sight of the people.

4:31 And the people believed. And when they heard that YHWH had visited the people of Yisra'EL and that he had seen their affliction, they bowed their heads and worshiped.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QExoda and 4QpaleoExodm preserve portions of Chapter 4. The signs given to Moshe and the declaration of Yisra'EL as YHWH's firstborn son at verse 4:22 are attested in the Cave 4 manuscripts.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 4 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in this chapter.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 4:22 — Yisra'EL is my son, my firstborn. YHWH declares the corporate identity of the covenant people before a single plague falls. The nation is YHWH's son. This is not a metaphor employed for rhetorical effect — it is a covenant legal category. The firstborn holds the inheritance rights. YHWH is asserting before Pharaoh that Yisra'EL has covenant standing as heir. The coming confrontation with Pharaoh is not a power contest between two rulers. It is a legal claim: YHWH demands the release of his firstborn son.

MOSHE BEFORE PHARAOH — THE TEN PLAGUES — CHAPTERS 5 THROUGH 12

SHEMOT — CHAPTER 5 Moshe and Aharon Before Pharaoh — The Burdens Increased

5:1 Afterward Moshe and Aharon went and said to Pharaoh: Thus says YHWH, The Creator of Yisra'EL — Let my people go, that they may hold a feast to me in the wilderness.

5:2 But Pharaoh said: Who is YHWH, that I should obey his voice and let Yisra'EL go? I do not know YHWH, and moreover, I will not let Yisra'EL go.

5:3 Then they said: The Creator of the Hebrews has met with us. Please let us go a three days' journey into the wilderness that we may sacrifice to YHWH our Creator, lest he fall upon us with pestilence or with the sword.

5:4 But the king of Egypt said to them: Moshe and Aharon, why do you take the people away from their work? Get back to your burdens.

5:6 The same day Pharaoh commanded the taskmasters of the people and their foremen:

5:7 You shall no longer give the people straw to make bricks, as in the past — let them go and gather straw for themselves.

5:8 But the number of bricks that they made in the past you shall impose on them — you shall by no means reduce it, for they are idle. Therefore they cry: Let us go and offer sacrifice to our Creator.

5:9 Let heavier work be laid on the men that they may labor at it and pay no attention to lying words.

5:17 But he said: You are idle, you are idle — that is why you say: Let us go and sacrifice to YHWH.

5:21 And they said to them: YHWH look on you and judge, because you have made us stink in the sight of Pharaoh and his servants, and have put a sword in their hand to kill us.

5:22 Then Moshe turned to YHWH and said: O YHWH, why have you done evil to this people? Why did you ever send me?

5:23 For since I came to Pharaoh to speak in your name, he has done evil to this people, and you have not delivered your people at all.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QExoda and 4QpaleoExodm preserve portions of Chapter 5.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 5 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in this chapter.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 5:2 — Who is YHWH, that I should obey his voice? Pharaoh's question is the theological question the entire plague sequence answers. He does not know YHWH. The ten plagues are not simply punishments — they are the answer to Pharaoh's question. Each plague demonstrates a specific domain of YHWH's authority over the natural order that the Egyptians attributed to their own deities. By the tenth plague, Pharaoh will know.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 5:22-23 — Moshe challenges YHWH directly: why have you done evil to this people? Why did you ever send me? Moshe's prayer is raw and unfiltered. The ancient record does not edit out the prophet's complaint. YHWH answers in Chapter 6 not with rebuke but with a renewed covenant declaration. The ancient Hebrew theology of prayer encompasses honest protest. YHWH is not fragile before Moshe's anger.

SHEMOT — CHAPTER 6 YHWH Renews the Covenant — The Genealogy of Moshe and Aharon

6:1 But YHWH said to Moshe: Now you shall see what I will do to Pharaoh — for with a strong hand he will send them out, and with a strong hand he will drive them out of his land.

6:2 The Creator spoke to Moshe and said to him: I am YHWH.

6:3 I appeared to Avraham, to Yitzhak, and to Yaakov, as The Creator Almighty, but by my name YHWH I did not make myself known to them.

6:4 I also established my covenant with them to give them the land of Kena'an, the land in which they lived as sojourners.

6:5 Moreover, I have heard the groaning of the people of Yisra'EL whom the Egyptians hold as slaves, and I have remembered my covenant.

6:6 Say therefore to the people of Yisra'EL: I am YHWH, and I will bring you out from under the burdens of the Egyptians, and I will deliver you from slavery to them, and I will redeem you with an outstretched arm and with great acts of judgment.

6:7 I will take you to be my people, and I will be your Creator, and you shall know that I am YHWH your Creator, who has brought you out from under the burdens of the Egyptians.

6:8 I will bring you into the land that I swore to give to Avraham, to Yitzhak, and to Yaakov. I will give it to you for a possession. I am YHWH.

6:9 Moshe spoke thus to the people of Yisra'EL, but they did not listen to Moshe, because of their broken spirit and harsh slavery.

6:12 But Moshe said to YHWH: Behold, the people of Yisra'EL have not listened to me. How then shall Pharaoh listen to me, for I am of uncircumcised lips?

6:14 These are the heads of their fathers' houses. The sons of Re'uven, the firstborn of Yisra'EL: Hanokh, Pallu, Hetzron, and Karmi — these are the clans of Re'uven.

6:15 The sons of Shim'on: Yemu'el, Yamin, Ohad, Yakhin, Tzohar, and Sha'ul, the son of a Kena'anite woman — these are the clans of Shim'on.

6:16 These are the names of the sons of Levi according to their generations: Gershon, Kehat, and Merari, the years of the life of Levi being 137 years.

6:18 The sons of Kehat: Amram, Yitzhar, Hebron, and Uzzi'el, the years of the life of Kehat being 133 years.

6:19 The sons of Merari: Mahli and Mushi. These are the clans of the Levites according to their generations.

6:20 Amram took as his wife Yokheved his father's sister, and she bore him Aharon and Moshe, the years of the life of Amram being 137 years.

6:23 Aharon took as his wife Elisheva, the daughter of Amminadav and the sister of Nahshon, and she bore him Nadav, Avihu, El'azar, and Itamar.

6:26 These are the Aharon and Moshe to whom YHWH said: Bring out the people of Yisra'EL from the land of Egypt by their hosts.

6:27 It was they who spoke to Pharaoh king of Egypt about bringing out the people of Yisra'EL from Egypt, this Moshe and this Aharon.

6:28 On the day when YHWH spoke to Moshe in the land of Egypt,

6:29 YHWH said to Moshe: I am YHWH — tell Pharaoh king of Egypt all that I say to you.

6:30 But Moshe said to YHWH: Behold, I am of uncircumcised lips. How will Pharaoh listen to me?

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QExoda and 4QpaleoExodm preserve portions of Chapter 6. The covenant renewal declaration — the seven I will statements at verses 6:6-8 — is attested in the Cave 4 manuscripts.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 6 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in this chapter.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verses 6:6-8 — I will bring you out, I will deliver you, I will redeem you, I will take you to be my people, I will be your Creator, you shall know that I am YHWH, I will bring you into the land. Seven covenant declarations in three verses. This is YHWH's covenant charter for the Exodus. Each statement is a covenant obligation YHWH places upon himself. The Exodus is not a favor. It is a covenant fulfillment. YHWH is discharging his own sworn word to Avraham, Yitzhak, and Yaakov.

SHEMOT — CHAPTER 7 Aharon's Staff — The First Plague — Blood

7:1 And YHWH said to Moshe: See, I have made you like a creator to Pharaoh, and your brother Aharon shall be your prophet.

7:2 You shall speak all that I command you, and your brother Aharon shall tell Pharaoh to let the people of Yisra'EL go out of his land.

7:3 But I will harden Pharaoh's heart, and though I multiply my signs and wonders in the land of Egypt,

7:4 Pharaoh will not listen to you. Then I will lay my hand on Egypt and bring my hosts, my people the children of Yisra'EL, out of the land of Egypt by great acts of judgment.

7:5 The Egyptians shall know that I am YHWH, when I stretch out my hand against Egypt and bring out the people of Yisra'EL from among them.

7:10 So Moshe and Aharon went to Pharaoh and did just as YHWH commanded. Aharon cast down his staff before Pharaoh and his servants, and it became a serpent.

7:11 Then Pharaoh summoned the wise men and the sorcerers, and they, the magicians of Egypt, also did the same by their secret arts.

7:12 For each man cast down his staff, and they became serpents. But Aharon's staff swallowed up their staffs.

7:13 Still Pharaoh's heart was hardened, and he would not listen to them, as YHWH had said.

7:14 Then YHWH said to Moshe: Pharaoh's heart is hardened — he refuses to let the people go.

7:17 Thus says YHWH: By this you shall know that I am YHWH — behold, with the staff that is in my hand I will strike the water that is in the Nile, and it shall turn into blood.

7:20 Moshe and Aharon did as YHWH commanded. In the sight of Pharaoh and in the sight of his servants he lifted up the staff and struck the water in the Nile, and all the water in the Nile turned into blood.

7:21 And the fish in the Nile died, and the Nile stank, so that the Egyptians could not drink water from the Nile. There was blood throughout all the land of Egypt.

7:22 But the magicians of Egypt did the same by their secret arts. So Pharaoh's heart remained hardened, and he would not listen to them, as YHWH had said.

7:23 Pharaoh turned and went into his house, and he did not take even this to heart.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QExoda, 4QExodb, and 4QpaleoExodm preserve portions of Chapter 7 including the plague of blood.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 7 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in this chapter.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 7:5 — the Egyptians shall know that I am YHWH. This phrase — you shall know that I am YHWH — is the recurring refrain throughout the plague sequence. It is spoken to Pharaoh, to Egypt, and to Yisra'EL. The plagues are a knowledge campaign. YHWH is answering Pharaoh's question from Chapter 5:2 — who is YHWH? — with ten demonstrations in escalating severity. The Nile was the life-source of Egyptian civilization and was considered sacred. Striking the Nile first is a direct confrontation with the theological heart of Egyptian religion.

SHEMOT — CHAPTER 8 Frogs — Gnats — Flies — The Second Third and Fourth Plagues

8:1 Then YHWH said to Moshe: Go in to Pharaoh and say to him: Thus says YHWH — Let my people go, that they may serve me.

8:2 But if you refuse to let them go, behold, I will plague all your country with frogs.

8:3 The Nile shall swarm with frogs that shall come up into your house and into your bedroom and on your bed and into the houses of your servants and your people, and into your ovens and your kneading bowls.

8:5 And YHWH said to Moshe: Say to Aharon — stretch out your hand with your staff over the rivers, over the canals and over the pools, and make frogs come up on the land of Egypt.

8:6 So Aharon stretched out his hand over the waters of Egypt, and the frogs came up and covered the land of Egypt.

8:8 Then Pharaoh called Moshe and Aharon and said: Plead with YHWH to take away the frogs from me and from my people, and I will let the people go to sacrifice to YHWH.

8:9 Moshe said to Pharaoh: Be pleased to command me when I am to plead for you and for your servants and for your people, that the frogs be cut off from you and your houses and be left only in the Nile.

8:10 And he said: Tomorrow. Moshe said: Be it as you say, so that you may know that there is no one like YHWH our Creator.

8:15 But when Pharaoh saw that there was a respite, he hardened his heart and would not listen to them, as YHWH had said.

8:16 Then YHWH said to Moshe: Say to Aharon — stretch out your staff and strike the dust of the earth, so that it may become gnats in all the land of Egypt.

8:17 And they did so. Aharon stretched out his hand with his staff and struck the dust of the earth, and there were gnats on man and beast. All the dust of the earth became gnats in all the land of Egypt.

8:18 The magicians tried by their secret arts to produce gnats, but they could not. So there were gnats on man and beast.

8:19 Then the magicians said to Pharaoh: This is the finger of The Creator. But Pharaoh's heart was hardened, and he would not listen to them, as YHWH had said.

8:20 Then YHWH said to Moshe: Rise up early in the morning and present yourself to Pharaoh, as he goes out to the water, and say to him: Thus says YHWH — Let my people go, that they may serve me.

8:22 But on that day I will set apart the land of Goshen, where my people dwell, so that no swarms of flies shall be there, that you may know that I am YHWH in the midst of the earth.

8:23 Thus I will put a division between my people and your people. Tomorrow this sign shall happen.

8:24 And YHWH did so. There came great swarms of flies into the house of Pharaoh and into his servants' houses. Throughout all the land of Egypt the land was ruined by the swarms of flies.

8:32 But Pharaoh hardened his heart this time also, and did not let the people go.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QExoda and 4QpaleoExodm preserve portions of Chapter 8. The distinction between Goshen and Egypt in the plague of flies — verse 8:22 — is attested in the DSS manuscripts.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 8 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QpaleoExodm has a longer text in Chapter 8, inserting additional material that brings the chapter into closer alignment with the account in Devarim 9. The DSS expanded text is noted as a significant variant.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 8:19 — the magicians said to Pharaoh: This is the finger of The Creator. The Egyptian magicians — Pharaoh's own court — acknowledge YHWH's direct action at the third plague. They cannot reproduce gnats. Their testimony is the most remarkable evidence in the plague sequence: YHWH's opponents in the Egyptian court confess his power before Pharaoh does. Pharaoh ignores his own advisors.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 8:22 — I will set apart the land of Goshen, where my people dwell. From the fourth plague onward, YHWH makes a categorical distinction between Egypt and Goshen. Yisra'EL's land is exempt. This is the covenant distinction made visible in nature itself. The plagues are not indiscriminate natural disasters. They are targeted, bounded, and purposeful — operating under covenant logic.

SHEMOT — CHAPTER 9 Livestock — Boils — Hail — Plagues Five Six and Seven

9:1 Then YHWH said to Moshe: Go in to Pharaoh and say to him: Thus says YHWH, The Creator of the Hebrews — Let my people go, that they may serve me.

9:3 Behold, the hand of YHWH will fall with a very severe plague upon your livestock that are in the field, the horses, the donkeys, the camels, the herds, and the flocks.

9:4 But YHWH will make a distinction between the livestock of Yisra'EL and the livestock of Egypt, so that nothing of all that belongs to the people of Yisra'EL shall die.

9:6 And the next day YHWH did this thing. All the livestock of the Egyptians died, but not one of the livestock of the people of Yisra'EL died.

9:7 And Pharaoh sent, and behold, not one of the livestock of Yisra'EL was dead. But the heart of Pharaoh was hardened, and he did not let the people go.

9:8 And YHWH said to Moshe and Aharon: Take handfuls of soot from the kiln, and let Moshe throw them in the air in the sight of Pharaoh.

9:9 It shall become fine dust over all the land of Egypt, and become boils breaking out in sores on man and beast throughout all the land of Egypt.

9:10 So they took soot from the kiln and stood before Pharaoh. And Moshe threw it in the air, and it became boils breaking out in sores on man and beast.

9:11 And the magicians could not stand before Moshe because of the boils, for the boils came upon the magicians and upon all the Egyptians.

9:12 But YHWH hardened the heart of Pharaoh, and he did not listen to them, as YHWH had spoken to Moshe.

9:13 Then YHWH said to Moshe: Rise up early in the morning and present yourself before Pharaoh and say to him: Thus says YHWH, The Creator of the Hebrews — Let my people go, that they may serve me.

9:14 For this time I will send all my plagues on you yourself, and on your servants and your people, so that you may know that there is none like me in all the earth.

9:16 But for this purpose I have raised you up, to show you my power, so that my name may be proclaimed in all the earth.

9:22 Then YHWH said to Moshe: Stretch out your hand toward heaven, so that there may be hail in all the land of Egypt, on man and beast and every plant of the field, in the land of Egypt.

9:24 There was hail and fire flashing continually in the midst of the hail, very heavy hail, such as had never been in all the land of Egypt since it became a nation.

9:25 The hail struck down everything that was in the field in all the land of Egypt, both man and beast. And the hail struck down every plant of the field and broke every tree of the field.

9:26 Only in the land of Goshen, where the people of Yisra'EL were, was there no hail.

9:27 Then Pharaoh sent and called Moshe and Aharon and said to them: This time I have sinned — YHWH is in the right, and I and my people are in the wrong.

9:28 Plead with YHWH, for there has been enough of The Creator's thunder and hail. I will let you go, and you shall stay no longer.

9:34 But when Pharaoh saw that the rain and the hail and the thunder had ceased, he sinned yet again and hardened his heart, he and his servants.

9:35 So the heart of Pharaoh was hardened, and he did not let the people of Yisra'EL go, as YHWH had spoken through Moshe.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QExoda, 4QExodc, and 4QpaleoExodm preserve portions of Chapter 9. The plague of hail and the exemption of Goshen are attested in the Cave 4 manuscripts.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 9 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QpaleoExodm again presents an expanded text in Chapter 9 at multiple points, particularly around the commands and fulfillments. The DSS expanded version is more detailed than the Masoretic in several verses.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 9:16 — for this purpose I have raised you up, to show you my power, so that my name may be proclaimed in all the earth. This verse is the theological key to the hardening of Pharaoh's heart throughout the plague sequence. YHWH states his purpose directly: the proclamation of his name in all the earth. The extended confrontation serves a global purpose beyond the liberation of Yisra'EL. Every nation is to know that YHWH acted in Egypt. The covenant with Avraham — in you all the families of the earth shall be blessed — operates even through judgment.

SHEMOT — CHAPTER 10 Locusts — Darkness — Plagues Eight and Nine

10:1 Then YHWH said to Moshe: Go in to Pharaoh, for I have hardened his heart and the heart of his servants, that I may show these signs of mine among them,

10:2 And that you may tell in the hearing of your son and of your grandson how I have dealt harshly with the Egyptians and what signs I have done among them, that you may know that I am YHWH.

10:3 So Moshe and Aharon went in to Pharaoh and said to him: Thus says YHWH, The Creator of the Hebrews — How long will you refuse to humble yourself before me? Let my people go, that they may serve me.

10:4 For if you refuse to let my people go, behold, tomorrow I will bring locusts into your country,

10:5 And they shall cover the face of the land, so that no one can see the land. And they shall eat what is left to you after the hail, and they shall eat every tree of yours that grows in the field,

10:7 Then Pharaoh's servants said to him: How long shall this man be a snare to us? Let the men go, that they may serve YHWH their Creator. Do you not yet understand that Egypt is ruined?

10:13 So Moshe stretched out his staff over the land of Egypt, and YHWH brought an east wind upon the land all that day and all that night. When it was morning, the east wind had brought the locusts.

10:14 The locusts came up over all the land of Egypt and settled on the whole country of Egypt, such a dense swarm of locusts as had never been before, nor ever will be again.

10:15 They covered the face of the whole land, so that the land was darkened, and they ate all the plants in the land and all the fruit of the trees that the hail had left. Not a green thing remained, neither tree nor plant of the field, through all the land of Egypt.

10:19 And YHWH turned the wind into a very strong west wind, which lifted the locusts and drove them into the Red Sea. Not a single locust was left in all the country of Egypt.

10:20 But YHWH hardened Pharaoh's heart, and he did not let the people of Yisra'EL go.

10:21 Then YHWH said to Moshe: Stretch out your hand toward heaven, that there may be darkness over the land of Egypt, a darkness to be felt.

10:22 So Moshe stretched out his hand toward heaven, and there was pitch darkness in all the land of Egypt three days.

10:23 They did not see one another. No one rose from his place for three days, but all the people of Yisra'EL had light where they lived.

10:24 Then Pharaoh called Moshe and said: Go, serve YHWH. Your little ones also may go with you — only let your flocks and your herds remain behind.

10:26 Our livestock also must go with us — not a hoof shall be left behind, for we must take of them to serve YHWH our Creator, and we do not know with what we must serve YHWH until we arrive there.

10:27 But YHWH hardened Pharaoh's heart, and he would not let them go.

10:28 Then Pharaoh said to him: Get away from me — take care never to see my face again, for on the day you see my face you shall die.

10:29 Moshe said: As you say — I will not see your face again.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QExoda and 4QpaleoExodm preserve portions of Chapter 10.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 10 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in this chapter.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 10:2 — that you may tell in the hearing of your son and of your grandson how I have dealt harshly with the Egyptians. The Exodus is designed for transmission across generations. YHWH explicitly builds the telling into the purpose of the event as it is happening. The covenant people are to be storytellers. The plagues, the Exodus, the crossing — these are not only history. They are a curriculum. Every generation is to know what YHWH did in Egypt.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 10:23 — all the people of Yisra'EL had light where they lived. The plague of darkness is total over Egypt but does not reach Goshen. Light and darkness are divided along covenant lines. This is Bereshit 1 language — the separation of light from darkness — applied to covenant history. YHWH who separated light from darkness at creation separates his people from Egypt's darkness in the ninth plague.

SHEMOT — CHAPTER 11 The Announcement of the Tenth Plague

11:1 YHWH said to Moshe: Yet one more plague I will bring upon Pharaoh and upon Egypt. Afterward he will let you go from here. When he lets you go, he will drive you away completely.

11:2 Speak now in the hearing of the people, that they ask, every man of his neighbor and every woman of her neighbor, for silver and gold jewelry.

11:3 And YHWH gave the people favor in the sight of the Egyptians. Moreover, the man Moshe was very great in the land of Egypt, in the sight of Pharaoh's servants and in the sight of the people.

11:4 So Moshe said: Thus says YHWH — About midnight I will go out in the midst of Egypt,

11:5 And every firstborn in the land of Egypt shall die, from the firstborn of Pharaoh who sits on his throne, even to the firstborn of the slave girl who is behind the hand mill, and all the firstborn of the livestock.

11:6 There shall be a great cry throughout all the land of Egypt, such as there has never been, nor ever will be again.

11:7 But not a dog shall growl against any of the people of Yisra'EL, either man or beast, that you may know that YHWH makes a distinction between Egypt and Yisra'EL.

11:8 And all these your servants shall come down to me and bow down to me, saying: Get out, you and all the people who follow you. And after that I will go out. And he went out from Pharaoh in hot anger.

11:9 Then YHWH said to Moshe: Pharaoh will not listen to you, that my wonders may be multiplied in the land of Egypt.

11:10 Moshe and Aharon did all these wonders before Pharaoh, and YHWH hardened Pharaoh's heart, and he did not let the people of Yisra'EL go out of his land.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QExoda and 4QpaleoExodm preserve portions of Chapter 11.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 11 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in this chapter.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 11:7 — not a dog shall growl against any of the people of Yisra'EL — that you may know that YHWH makes a distinction between Egypt and Yisra'EL. The distinction is total. Even animals respond differently around the covenant people on the night of the tenth plague. YHWH does not make a partial distinction. The boundary is absolute. The ancient record uses this image — not a single dog growling — to illustrate the completeness of YHWH's covenant protection on that night.

SHEMOT — CHAPTER 12 The Pesach — The Death of the Firstborn — The Exodus Begins

12:1 YHWH said to Moshe and Aharon in the land of Egypt:

12:2 This month shall be for you the beginning of months. It shall be the first month of the year for you.

12:3 Tell all the congregation of Yisra'EL that on the tenth day of this month every man shall take a lamb according to their fathers' houses, a lamb for a household.

12:5 Your lamb shall be without blemish, a male a year old. You may take it from the sheep or from the goats,

12:6 And you shall keep it until the fourteenth day of this month, when the whole assembly of the congregation of Yisra'EL shall kill their lambs at twilight.

12:7 Then they shall take some of the blood and put it on the two doorposts and the lintel of the houses in which they eat it.

12:8 They shall eat the flesh that night, roasted on the fire — with unleavened bread and bitter herbs they shall eat it.

12:11 In this manner you shall eat it — with your belt fastened, your sandals on your feet, and your staff in your hand. And you shall eat it in haste. It is YHWH's Pesach.

12:12 For I will pass through the land of Egypt that night, and I will strike all the firstborn in the land of Egypt, both man and beast. And on all the deities of Egypt I will execute judgments — I am YHWH.

12:13 The blood shall be a sign for you, on the houses where you are. And when I see the blood, I will pass over you, and no plague will befall you to destroy you, when I strike the land of Egypt.

12:14 This day shall be for you a memorial day, and you shall keep it as a feast to YHWH — throughout your generations, as a statute forever, you shall keep it as a feast.

12:17 And you shall observe the Feast of Unleavened Bread, for on this very day I brought your hosts out of the land of Egypt. Therefore you shall observe this day, throughout your generations, as a statute forever.

12:21 Then Moshe called all the elders of Yisra'EL and said to them: Go and select lambs for yourselves according to your clans, and kill the Pesach lamb.

12:22 Take a bunch of hyssop and dip it in the blood that is in the basin, and touch the lintel and the two doorposts with the blood that is in the basin. None of you shall go out of the door of his house until the morning.

12:23 For YHWH will pass through to strike the Egyptians, and when he sees the blood on the lintel and on the two doorposts, YHWH will pass over the door and will not allow the destroyer to enter your houses to strike you.

12:24 You shall observe this rite as a statute for you and for your sons forever.

12:28 Then the people of Yisra'EL went and did so — as YHWH had commanded Moshe and Aharon, so they did.

12:29 At midnight YHWH struck down all the firstborn in the land of Egypt, from the firstborn of Pharaoh who sat on his throne to the firstborn of the captive who was in the dungeon, and all the firstborn of the livestock.

12:30 And Pharaoh rose up in the night, he and all his servants and all the Egyptians. And there was a great cry in Egypt, for there was not a house where someone was not dead.

12:31 Then he summoned Moshe and Aharon by night and said: Up, go out from among my people, both you and the people of Yisra'EL. And go, serve YHWH, as you have said.

12:33 And the Egyptians were urgent with the people to send them out of the land in haste. For they said: We shall all be dead.

12:36 And YHWH had given the people favor in the sight of the Egyptians, so that they let them have what they asked. Thus they plundered the Egyptians.

12:37 And the people of Yisra'EL journeyed from Ra'amses to Sukkot, about six hundred thousand men on foot, besides women and children.

12:38 A mixed multitude also went up with them, and very much livestock, both flocks and herds.

12:40 The time that the people of Yisra'EL lived in Egypt was 430 years.

12:41 At the end of 430 years, on that very day, all the hosts of YHWH went out from the land of Egypt.

12:42 It was a night of watching by YHWH, to bring them out of the land of Egypt. So this same night is a night of watching kept to YHWH by all the people of Yisra'EL throughout their generations.

12:46 It shall be eaten in one house — you shall not take any of the flesh outside the house, and you shall not break any of its bones.

12:48 If a stranger shall sojourn with you and would keep the Pesach to YHWH, let all his males be circumcised. Then he may come near and keep it — he shall be as a native of the land. But no uncircumcised person shall eat of it.

12:50 All the people of Yisra'EL did just as YHWH commanded Moshe and Aharon.

12:51 And on that very day YHWH brought the people of Yisra'EL out of the land of Egypt by their hosts.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QExoda, 4QExodb, 4QExodc, and 4QpaleoExodm preserve portions of Chapter 12. The Pesach ordinances and the account of the tenth plague are among the most extensively preserved passages in the Qumran Exodus manuscripts. The requirement that no bone of the Pesach lamb be broken — verse 12:46 — is attested in multiple DSS manuscripts.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 12 in full. The Orit and the Book of Yovelim (49:1-23) give extensive treatment to the Pesach — Yovelim declares it an eternal ordinance written on the heavenly tablets.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in this chapter.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 12:12 — on all the deities of Egypt I will execute judgments — I am YHWH. The tenth plague is explicitly framed as judgment against the deities of Egypt, not merely against Pharaoh or the Egyptian people. Each of the ten plagues targeted a specific domain of Egyptian religious life — the Nile, the earth, the sky, the sun — domains attributed to specific Egyptian deities. The death of the firstborn, including the firstborn of Pharaoh who was himself considered divine, is the final and comprehensive repudiation of Egyptian theology. YHWH executes judgment against every power Egypt placed in the position of deity.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 12:38 — a mixed multitude also went up with them. The Exodus is not ethnically exclusive in the ancient record. A mixed multitude — erev rav — left Egypt alongside the children of Yisra'EL. Non-Hebrews who had seen YHWH's power and chose to leave Egypt with the covenant people are included in the Exodus. The covenant community from the beginning was open to those who joined it — verse 12:48 makes circumcision the entry requirement, not ethnicity.

SHEMOT — CHAPTER 13 Consecration of the Firstborn — The Route of the Exodus — The Pillar

13:1 YHWH said to Moshe:

13:2 Consecrate to me all the firstborn. Whatever is the first to open the womb among the people of Yisra'EL, both of man and of beast, is mine.

13:3 Then Moshe said to the people: Remember this day in which you came out from Egypt, out of the house of slavery, for by a strong hand YHWH brought you out from this place. No leavened bread shall be eaten.

13:8 You shall tell your son on that day: It is because of what YHWH did for me when I came out of Egypt.

13:9 And it shall be to you as a sign on your hand and as a memorial between your eyes, that the law of YHWH may be in your mouth. For with a strong hand YHWH has brought you out of Egypt.

13:14 And when in time to come your son asks you: What does this mean? — you shall say to him: By a strong hand YHWH brought us out of Egypt, from the house of slavery.

13:17 When Pharaoh let the people go, The Creator did not lead them by way of the land of the Philistines, although that was near. For The Creator said: Lest the people change their minds when they see war and return to Egypt.

13:18 But The Creator led the people around by the way of the wilderness toward the Red Sea. And the people of Yisra'EL went up out of the land of Egypt equipped for battle.

13:19 Moshe took the bones of Yosef with him, for Yosef had made the sons of Yisra'EL solemnly swear, saying: The Creator will surely visit you, and you shall carry up my bones with you from here.

13:21 And YHWH went before them by day in a pillar of cloud to lead them along the way, and by night in a pillar of fire to give them light, that they might travel by day and by night.

13:22 The pillar of cloud by day and the pillar of fire by night did not depart from before the people.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QExoda and 4QpaleoExodm preserve portions of Chapter 13. The pillar of cloud and fire are attested in the Cave 4 manuscripts.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 13 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in this chapter.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 13:19 — Moshe took the bones of Yosef with him. The oath sworn by Yosef in Bereshit 50:25 is honored here, two books later, at the moment of the Exodus. The bones of Yosef travel the entire wilderness journey. They are buried at Shechem in Yehoshua 24:32 — completing a journey that began with Yosef being thrown into a pit near Shechem in Bereshit 37. The Torah's threads span centuries and books. Nothing is forgotten.

THE CROSSING OF THE SEA — SINAI — CHAPTERS 14 THROUGH 18

SHEMOT — CHAPTER 14 Pharaoh Pursues — The Crossing of the Sea — Egypt Destroyed

14:1 Then YHWH said to Moshe:

14:2 Tell the people of Yisra'EL to turn back and encamp in front of Pi-Hahirot, between Migdol and the sea, in front of Ba'al-Tzefon — you shall encamp facing it, by the sea.

14:3 For Pharaoh will say of the people of Yisra'EL: They are wandering in the land — the wilderness has shut them in.

14:4 And I will harden Pharaoh's heart, and he will pursue them, and I will get glory over Pharaoh and all his host, and the Egyptians shall know that I am YHWH. And they did so.

14:5 When the king of Egypt was told that the people had fled, the mind of Pharaoh and his servants was changed toward the people, and they said: What is this we have done, that we have let Yisra'EL go from serving us?

14:6 So he made ready his chariot and took his army with him,

14:7 And took six hundred chosen chariots and all the other chariots of Egypt with officers over all of them.

14:8 And YHWH hardened the heart of Pharaoh king of Egypt, and he pursued the people of Yisra'EL while the people of Yisra'EL were going out defiantly.

14:10 When Pharaoh drew near, the people of Yisra'EL lifted up their eyes, and behold, the Egyptians were marching after them, and they feared greatly. And the people of Yisra'EL cried out to YHWH.

14:11 They said to Moshe: Is it because there are no graves in Egypt that you have taken us away to die in the wilderness? What have you done to us in bringing us out of Egypt?

14:13 And Moshe said to the people: Fear not, stand firm, and see the salvation of YHWH, which he will work for you today. For the Egyptians whom you see today, you shall never see again.

14:14 YHWH will fight for you, and you have only to be silent.

14:15 YHWH said to Moshe: Why do you cry to me? Tell the people of Yisra'EL to go forward.

14:16 Lift up your staff, and stretch out your hand over the sea and divide it, that the people of Yisra'EL may go through the sea on dry ground.

14:19 Then the angel of The Creator who was going before the host of Yisra'EL moved and went behind them, and the pillar of cloud moved from before them and stood behind them,

14:20 Coming between the host of Egypt and the host of Yisra'EL. And there was the cloud and the darkness. And it lit up the night without one coming near the other all night.

14:21 Then Moshe stretched out his hand over the sea, and YHWH drove the sea back by a strong east wind all night and made the sea dry land, and the waters were divided.

14:22 And the people of Yisra'EL went into the midst of the sea on dry ground, the waters being a wall to them on their right hand and on their left.

14:23 The Egyptians pursued and went in after them into the midst of the sea, all Pharaoh's horses, his chariots, and his horsemen.

14:26 Then YHWH said to Moshe: Stretch out your hand over the sea, that the water may come back upon the Egyptians, upon their chariots, and upon their horsemen.

14:27 So Moshe stretched out his hand over the sea, and the sea returned to its normal course when the morning appeared. And as the Egyptians fled into it, YHWH threw the Egyptians into the midst of the sea.

14:28 The waters returned and covered the chariots and the horsemen — all the host of Pharaoh that had followed them into the sea. Not one of them remained.

14:29 But the people of Yisra'EL walked on dry ground through the sea, the waters being a wall to them on their right hand and on their left.

14:30 Thus YHWH saved Yisra'EL that day from the hand of the Egyptians, and Yisra'EL saw the Egyptians dead on the seashore.

14:31 Yisra'EL saw the great power that YHWH used against the Egyptians, so the people feared YHWH, and they believed in YHWH and in his servant Moshe.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QExoda, 4QExodb, and 4QpaleoExodm preserve portions of Chapter 14. The crossing of the sea is one of the most attested events in the Qumran Exodus manuscripts.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 14 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in this chapter.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 14:14 — YHWH will fight for you, and you have only to be silent. This is the most concentrated statement of covenant rescue in all of Shemot. The people are trapped — sea before them, Egyptian army behind them. Moshe's answer is not a battle plan. It is a theology: stand firm, be silent, watch. YHWH fights. The covenant people's role at the sea is to witness, not to act. This is the inverse of what is required at Sinai and in the wilderness — but at the sea, the battle is entirely YHWH's.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 14:31 — the people feared YHWH, and they believed in YHWH and in his servant Moshe. The crossing of the sea produces faith. Not the burning bush. Not the plagues. Not the Pesach night. The final act of seeing the Egyptian army destroyed while walking on dry ground — that is what the ancient record identifies as the moment the people believed. Seeing YHWH's salvation completed moves the people from fear to faith.

SHEMOT — CHAPTER 15 The Song of the Sea — Miryam — Marah and Elim

15:1 Then Moshe and the people of Yisra'EL sang this song to YHWH, saying: I will sing to YHWH, for he has triumphed gloriously — the horse and his rider he has thrown into the sea.

15:2 YHWH is my strength and my song, and he has become my salvation — this is my Creator, and I will praise him, my father's Creator, and I will exalt him.

15:3 YHWH is a man of war — YHWH is his name.

15:4 Pharaoh's chariots and his host he cast into the sea, and his chosen officers were sunk in the Red Sea.

15:5 The floods covered them — they went down into the depths like a stone.

15:6 Your right hand, O YHWH, glorious in power — your right hand, O YHWH, shatters the enemy.

15:7 In the greatness of your majesty you overthrow your adversaries — you send out your fury — it consumes them like stubble.

15:8 At the blast of your nostrils the waters piled up — the floods stood up in a heap — the deeps congealed in the heart of the sea.

15:9 The enemy said: I will pursue, I will overtake, I will divide the spoil — my desire shall have its fill of them. I will draw my sword — my hand shall destroy them.

15:10 You blew with your wind — the sea covered them. They sank like lead in the mighty waters.

15:11 Who is like you, O YHWH, among the deities? Who is like you, majestic in holiness, awesome in glorious deeds, doing wonders?

15:12 You stretched out your right hand — the earth swallowed them.

15:13 You have led in your steadfast love the people whom you have redeemed — you have guided them by your strength to your holy abode.

15:17 You will bring them in and plant them on your own mountain, the place, O YHWH, which you have made for your abode, the sanctuary, O YHWH, which your hands have established.

15:18 YHWH will reign forever and ever.

15:20 Then Miryam the prophet, the sister of Aharon, took a tambourine in her hand, and all the women went out after her with tambourines and dancing.

15:21 And Miryam sang to them: Sing to YHWH, for he has triumphed gloriously — the horse and his rider he has thrown into the sea.

15:22 Then Moshe made Yisra'EL set out from the Red Sea, and they went into the wilderness of Shur. They went three days in the wilderness and found no water.

15:23 When they came to Marah, they could not drink the water of Marah because it was bitter — therefore it was named Marah.

15:24 And the people grumbled against Moshe, saying: What shall we drink?

15:25 And he cried to YHWH, and YHWH showed him a log, and he threw it into the water, and the water became sweet. There YHWH made for them a statute and a rule, and there he tested them,

15:26 Saying: If you will diligently listen to the voice of YHWH your Creator, and do that which is right in his eyes, and give ear to his commandments and keep all his statutes, I will put none of the diseases on you that I put on the Egyptians, for I am YHWH your healer.

15:27 Then they came to Elim, where there were twelve springs of water and seventy palm trees, and they encamped there by the water.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QExoda and 4QpaleoExodm preserve portions of Chapter 15. The Song of the Sea is attested in the Cave 4 manuscripts. This is one of the oldest poetic texts in the Torah and is recognized by scholars as among the most ancient Hebrew literary compositions in the entire corpus.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 15 in full including the full Song of the Sea and Miryam's response.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in the Song of the Sea.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 15:20 — Miryam the prophet, the sister of Aharon. Miryam is named a prophet — neviah — here. She is one of three prophets named in the Exodus account — Moshe, Aharon, and Miryam. Her prophetic role is established at the moment of the greatest national celebration in the Torah. She leads the women in song and dance. The ancient record does not diminish her role. Mikvam 6:4 names all three as the leaders sent before Yisra'EL — Moshe, Aharon, and Miryam.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 15:11 — who is like you, O YHWH, among the deities? Mi khamokha ba-elim YHWH. The Song of the Sea does not deny the existence of other spiritual powers. It asserts YHWH's incomparable superiority among them. This is the ancient Hebrew theological position preserved consistently throughout the Torah and the prophetic literature: YHWH is not one among equals but incomparable — majestic in holiness, awesome in glorious deeds, doing wonders.

SHEMOT — CHAPTER 16 The Manna — The Quail — The Sabbath in the Wilderness

16:1 They set out from Elim, and all the congregation of the people of Yisra'EL came to the wilderness of Sin, which is between Elim and Sinai, on the fifteenth day of the second month after they had departed from the land of Egypt.

16:2 And the whole congregation of the people of Yisra'EL grumbled against Moshe and Aharon in the wilderness,

16:3 And the people of Yisra'EL said to them: Would that we had died by the hand of YHWH in the land of Egypt, when we sat by the meat pots and ate bread to the full, for you have brought us out into this wilderness to kill this whole assembly with hunger.

16:4 Then YHWH said to Moshe: Behold, I am about to rain bread from heaven for you, and the people shall go out and gather a day's portion every day, that I may test them, whether they will walk in my law or not.

16:5 On the sixth day, when they prepare what they bring in, it will be twice as much as they gather daily.

16:11 And YHWH said to Moshe:

16:12 I have heard the grumbling of the people of Yisra'EL. Say to them: At twilight you shall eat meat, and in the morning you shall be filled with bread. Then you shall know that I am YHWH your Creator.

16:13 In the evening quail came up and covered the camp, and in the morning dew lay around the camp.

16:14 And when the dew had gone up, there was on the face of the wilderness a fine, flake-like thing, fine as frost on the ground.

16:15 When the people of Yisra'EL saw it, they said to one another: What is it? For they did not know what it was. And Moshe said to them: It is the bread that YHWH has given you to eat.

16:16 This is what YHWH has commanded: Gather of it, each one of you, as much as he can eat.

16:19 And Moshe said to them: Let no one leave any of it over till the morning.

16:20 But they did not listen to Moshe. Some left part of it till the morning, and it bred worms and stank. And Moshe was angry with them.

16:21 Morning by morning they gathered it, each as much as he could eat. But when the sun grew hot, it melted.

16:22 On the sixth day they gathered twice as much bread, two omers each. And when all the leaders of the congregation came and told Moshe,

16:23 He said to them: This is what YHWH has commanded — tomorrow is a day of solemn rest, a holy Sabbath to YHWH. Bake what you will bake and boil what you will boil, and all that is left over lay aside to be kept till the morning.

16:25 Moshe said: Eat it today, for today is a Sabbath to YHWH — today you will not find it in the field.

16:26 Six days you shall gather it, but on the seventh day, which is a Sabbath, there will be none.

16:28 And YHWH said to Moshe: How long will you refuse to keep my commandments and my laws?

16:29 See. YHWH has given you the Sabbath — therefore on the sixth day he gives you bread for two days. Remain each of you in his place — let no one go out of his place on the seventh day.

16:30 So the people rested on the seventh day.

16:31 Now the house of Yisra'EL called its name manna. It was like coriander seed, white, and the taste of it was like wafers made with honey.

16:33 And Moshe said to Aharon: Take a jar, and put an omer of manna in it, and place it before YHWH to be kept throughout your generations.

16:35 The people of Yisra'EL ate the manna forty years, till they came to a habitable land. They ate the manna till they came to the border of the land of Kena'an.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QExoda and 4QpaleoExodm preserve portions of Chapter 16. The manna account and the Sabbath legislation in the wilderness are attested in the Cave 4 manuscripts.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 16 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in this chapter.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 16:4 — I will test them, whether they will walk in my law or not. The manna is not only provision — it is a test. YHWH designs the daily dependence on manna to teach a specific lesson: reliance on YHWH one day at a time, with double provision before the Sabbath built into the design of the bread itself. The Sabbath is embedded into the manna before it is legislated at Sinai. YHWH teaches the Sabbath through the structure of provision before he commands it through the structure of law.

SHEMOT — CHAPTER 17 Water from the Rock at Massah — The Battle with Amalek

17:1 All the congregation of the people of Yisra'EL moved on from the wilderness of Sin by stages, according to the commandment of YHWH, and camped at Refidim, but there was no water for the people to drink.

17:2 Therefore the people quarreled with Moshe and said: Give us water to drink. And Moshe said to them: Why do you quarrel with me? Why do you test YHWH?

17:3 But the people thirsted there for water, and the people grumbled against Moshe and said: Why did you bring us up out of Egypt, to kill us and our children and our livestock with thirst?

17:4 So Moshe cried to YHWH: What shall I do with this people? They are almost ready to stone me.

17:5 And YHWH said to Moshe: Pass on before the people, taking with you some of the elders of Yisra'EL, and take in your hand the staff with which you struck the Nile, and go.

17:6 Behold, I will stand before you there on the rock at Horev, and you shall strike the rock, and water shall come out of it, and the people will drink. And Moshe did so, in the sight of the elders of Yisra'EL.

17:7 And he called the name of the place Massah and Merivah, because of the quarreling of the people of Yisra'EL, and because they tested YHWH by saying: Is YHWH among us or not?

17:8 Then Amalek came and fought with Yisra'EL at Refidim.

17:9 So Moshe said to Yehoshua: Choose for us men, and go out and fight with Amalek. Tomorrow I will stand on the top of the hill with the staff of The Creator in my hand.

17:10 So Yehoshua did as Moshe told him, and fought with Amalek, while Moshe, Aharon, and Hur went up to the top of the hill.

17:11 Whenever Moshe held up his hand, Yisra'EL prevailed, and whenever he lowered his hand, Amalek prevailed.

17:12 But Moshe's hands grew weary, so they took a stone and put it under him, and he sat on it, while Aharon and Hur held up his hands, one on one side, and the other on the other side. So his hands were steady until the going down of the sun.

17:13 And Yehoshua overwhelmed Amalek and his people with the sword.

17:14 Then YHWH said to Moshe: Write this as a memorial in a book and recite it in the ears of Yehoshua, that I will utterly blot out the memory of Amalek from under heaven.

17:15 And Moshe built an altar and called the name of it: YHWH is my banner,

17:16 Saying: A hand upon the throne of YHWH — YHWH will have war with Amalek from generation to generation.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QExoda and 4QpaleoExodm preserve portions of Chapter 17. The battle with Amalek and the command to write a memorial are attested in the Cave 4 manuscripts.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 17 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in this chapter.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 17:14 — write this as a memorial in a book. This is one of the explicit Torah commands to write. YHWH commands written record-keeping of the battle with Amalek. The Torah itself references its own composition — YHWH instructs Moshe to write at multiple points throughout Shemot, Bamidbar, and Devarim. The written Torah is not an external editorial project imposed after the fact. It is commanded in the text of the Torah itself.

SHEMOT — CHAPTER 18 Yitro Visits Moshe — The Appointment of Judges

18:1 Yitro, the priest of Midyan, Moshe's father-in-law, heard of all that The Creator had done for Moshe and for Yisra'EL his people, how YHWH had brought Yisra'EL out of Egypt.

18:2 Now Yitro, Moshe's father-in-law, had taken Tzipporah, Moshe's wife, after he had sent her home,

18:5 And Yitro, Moshe's father-in-law, came with his sons and his wife to Moshe in the wilderness where he was encamped at the mountain of The Creator.

18:7 Moshe went out to meet his father-in-law and bowed down and kissed him. And they asked each other of their welfare and went into the tent.

18:8 Then Moshe told his father-in-law all that YHWH had done to Pharaoh and to the Egyptians for Yisra'EL's sake, all the hardship that had come upon them in the way, and how YHWH had delivered them.

18:9 And Yitro rejoiced for all the good that YHWH had done to Yisra'EL, in that he had delivered them out of the hand of the Egyptians.

18:10 Yitro said: Blessed be YHWH, who has delivered you out of the hand of the Egyptians and out of the hand of Pharaoh and has delivered the people from under the hand of the Egyptians.

18:11 Now I know that YHWH is greater than all deities, because in this affair they dealt arrogantly with the people.

18:12 And Yitro, Moshe's father-in-law, brought a burnt offering and sacrifices to The Creator. And Aharon came with all the elders of Yisra'EL to eat bread with Moshe's father-in-law before The Creator.

18:13 The next day Moshe sat to judge the people, and the people stood around Moshe from morning till evening.

18:14 When Moshe's father-in-law saw all that he was doing for the people, he said: What is this that you are doing for the people? Why do you sit alone, and all the people stand around you from morning till evening?

18:17 Moshe's father-in-law said to him: What you are doing is not good.

18:18 You and the people with you will certainly wear yourselves out, for the thing is too heavy for you. You are not able to do it alone.

18:19 Now obey my voice — I will give you advice, and The Creator be with you. You shall represent the people before The Creator and bring their cases to The Creator,

18:21 Moreover, look for able men from all the people, men who fear The Creator, who are trustworthy and hate a bribe, and place such men over the people as chiefs of thousands, of hundreds, of fifties, and of tens.

18:22 And let them judge the people at all times. Every great matter they shall bring to you, but any small matter they shall decide themselves. So it will be easier for you, and they will bear the burden with you.

18:24 So Moshe listened to the voice of his father-in-law and did all that he had said.

18:25 Moshe chose able men out of all Yisra'EL and made them heads over the people, chiefs of thousands, of hundreds, of fifties, and of tens.

18:27 Then Moshe let his father-in-law depart, and he went away to his own country.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QExoda and 4QpaleoExodm preserve portions of Chapter 18. The account of Yitro's visit and the establishment of the judicial system are attested in the Cave 4 manuscripts.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 18 in full.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in this chapter.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 18:11 — now I know that YHWH is greater than all deities. Yitro — a Midyanite priest, not an Yisra'ELi — makes this declaration after hearing Moshe's account of the Exodus. He then brings burnt offerings and sacrifices to The Creator. This is the second time in Shemot that a non-Yisra'ELi recognizes YHWH's supremacy — the Egyptian magicians were the first. The ancient record documents YHWH's reputation spreading to the nations through the testimony of what he did in Egypt. Yitro's confession is the fulfillment of what YHWH declared in 9:16 — that his name would be proclaimed in all the earth.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 18:17-22 — the judicial structure proposed by Yitro and accepted by Moshe. The administration of covenant justice is not invented by Moshe alone — it comes from his Midyanite father-in-law through counsel. YHWH's wisdom for organizing covenant governance comes through a non-Hebrew voice. The ancient record is not provincial about where practical wisdom originates. Moshe submits to Yitro's counsel and implements it. The covenant community is structured partly through the insight of an outsider who feared The Creator.
